sections.paragraph:
-

(1) to pour out, to empty, prop. a vessel; see בַּקְבּוּק . (Arab. بَقْبَقَ onomatopoetic from the sound of a bottle when emptied, like the Pers. غلغل gulgul, Engl. to bubble. In the Maltese bakbak is, to bubble, boil up, as water, like the Arab. بعع, بغبغ, bokka, a bubble of water, bakbŷka, bekbŷka, a bottle; compare also בּוּעַ, בַּעְבַּע; בּוּק.) Figuratively-(a) to empty a land, depopulate it, Isa 24:1 to despoil, to pillage the inhabitants, Nah 2:3.-(b) Jer 19:7, בַּקֹּתִי אֶת־עֲצַת יְהוּדָה “I will empty, or pour out the counsel of Judah,” i.e. I will make them void of counsel. Compare Niphal, Isa 19:3.

(2) intrans. to be poured out, to be spread wide, used of a spreading tree; Hos 10:1, גֶּפֶן בֹּקֵק “a wide spreading vine;” LXX. ἄμπελος εὐκληματοῦσα: Vulg. frondosa.

Niphal נָבַק; inf. הִבֹּוק; fut. יִבֹּוק.-

(1) pass, of Kal No. 1, a, Isa 24:3.-(2) pass. of No. 1, b, Isa 19:3, נָֽבְקָה רוּחַ מִצְרַיִם מִקִּרְבֹּו “the spirit of Egypt shall be poured out from her midst,” i.e. she shall be altogether bereft of understanding and prudence; נָֽבְקָה is for נָבַקָּה; Lehrg. 372.

Poel בֹּוקֵק i.q. Kal 1, a, to depopulate, Jer 51:2.

Derived nouns are בַּקְבּוּק and pr.n. בַּקְבֻּקְיָה, בֻּקִּי, בֻּקִיָּהוּ, יַבֹּק.
